questions.q: The $j$-invariant is the unique $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$-invariant holomorphic function on $\mathcal{H}$ whose Fourier expansion begins:
questions.explain: $j(\tau) = q^{-1} + 744 + 196884\,q + 21493760\,q^2 + \cdots$ with $q = e^{2\pi i\tau}$. The pole is at the cusp $q=0$; the constant term $744$ is a normalization; the higher coefficients are the McKay–Thompson data.

questions.q: What is the coefficient of $q$ (i.e., $c_1$) in the Fourier expansion of $j(\tau)$?
questions.explain: $c_1 = 196884$. Numerically one unit more than the dimension $196883$ of the smallest non-trivial irreducible representation of the Monster — McKay's starting observation.

choices: a different pole at the cusp
vanishing constant term and otherwise the same coefficients
different coefficients of $q$ and $q^2$
different pole order at $i\infty$
questions.hint: Subtracting $744$ only changes the constant term; the pole $q^{-1}$ and every $c_n$ for $n \ge 1$ are unchanged.
questions.explain: Subtracting $744$ only changes the constant term; the pole $q^{-1}$ and every $c_n$ for $n \ge 1$ are unchanged. $J$ is the version whose graded-dimension statement, $J = \sum_{n} (\dim V^\natural_n) q^n$, is cleanest.

hard.q: The $j$-invariant is characterised as the Hauptmodul for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})\backslash\mathcal{H}^*$. Which property is the correct chain of deductions establishing uniqueness (up to additive constant)?
choices: The modular curve has genus $1$, so the function field has one generator; normalise the pole at the cusp to get uniqueness

The modular curve $X(1)$ has genus $0$ and exactly one cusp; any holomorphic function with a simple pole at the cusp and no other poles is determined up to $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{C})$ of the target; fixing the pole residue uniquely pins $j$
$\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ is a free group, so invariant functions are freely generated
Riemann–Roch forces every modular function to be a polynomial in $j$; uniqueness follows from minimal degree
hard.hint: The Hauptmodul is a generator for the function field of the quotient.
hard.explain: $X(1)=\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})\backslash\mathcal{H}^*$ has genus $0$ and one cusp. Genus $0$ means the function field is generated by a single element; choosing a simple pole at the cusp (Hauptmodul) with residue $1$ normalises away the $\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{P}^1)$ freedom, giving a unique choice up to an additive constant fixed by $c_0=744$.

hard.q: Compute $c_2 - c_1$ where $c_n$ denotes the $q^n$-coefficient of $j(\tau)$. (Use $c_1=196884$ and $c_2=21493760$.)
hard.explain: $c_2 - c_1 = 21493760 - 196884 = 21296876$, which is exactly $\dim V_3$, the third-smallest Monster irreducible — illustrating how Monster representation dimensions are encoded in differences of $j$-coefficients.

hard.q: Which of the following is a valid counterexample showing that $j(\tau)$ is NOT a modular form of positive integer weight for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$?
choices: $j(\tau)$ has a pole at the cusp $i\infty$, but holomorphic modular forms of weight $k>0$ are required to be holomorphic at the cusp
$j(\tau)$ transforms with a non-trivial multiplier system
$j(\tau)$ fails the Hecke-eigenform condition
$j(\tau)$ is not square-integrable on the fundamental domain
hard.hint: Positive-weight modular forms vanish at $\infty$ unless they are constants.
hard.explain: Holomorphic modular forms of positive weight must be holomorphic (including at cusps). The $j$-invariant has a simple pole $q^{-1}$ at the cusp $i\infty$, violating this condition; it is a meromorphic modular function of weight $0$, not a positive-weight form.

monster-group-structure.title: The Monster group and its irreducibles

choices: the largest alternating group
a Lie group of type $E_8$
the largest sporadic finite simple group
an infinite-dimensional Lie algebra
questions.hint: $\mathbb{M}$ is the largest of the $26$ sporadic finite simple groups, of order $\approx 8\times 10^{53}$.
questions.explain: $\mathbb{M}$ is the largest of the $26$ sporadic finite simple groups, of order $\approx 8\times 10^{53}$. The other finite simple groups sit in infinite families (cyclic of prime order, alternating, Lie type); $\mathbb{M}$ does not.

questions.q: What is the dimension of the smallest faithful (i.e., non-trivial) complex irreducible representation of the Monster?
questions.explain: $\dim V_2 = 196883$. The Griess algebra on which $\mathbb{M}$ was originally constructed is $196883$-dimensional (its identity element adds one more, giving the $196884$-dimensional 'Griess algebra plus identity' often quoted).

choices: all primes up to $71$
exactly the Fermat primes
exactly the Mersenne primes
questions.hint: These $15$ primes — the 'supersingular primes' in the sense of Ogg — are exactly the primes $p$ for which $\Gamma_0(p)+$ has genus $0$.
questions.explain: These $15$ primes — the 'supersingular primes' in the sense of Ogg — are exactly the primes $p$ for which $\Gamma_0(p)+$ has genus $0$. Ogg conjectured this coincidence before moonshine existed, and Conway–Norton's conjecture explained it.

hard.q: The Monster was predicted independently by Fischer and Griess in 1973 before its construction. Which step in Griess's 1980 construction is the decisive one that proves $\mathbb{M}$ exists?
choices: Proving a character table consistent with a simple group of the conjectured order exists
Explicitly constructing a $196884$-dimensional commutative non-associative algebra (the Griess algebra) and verifying it admits a group of automorphisms isomorphic to $\mathbb{M}$
Embedding $\mathbb{M}$ as a subgroup of $E_8(\mathbb{C})$
Applying the classification of finite simple groups to rule out all other candidates
hard.hint: The 196884-dimensional module plus algebra structure comes before the symmetry check.
hard.explain: Existence of a finite simple group requires an explicit construction. Griess built the $196884$-dimensional commutative algebra, computed its automorphism group, and verified it is simple of the correct order — this is the constructive proof of existence.

hard.q: The Monster has exactly $194$ conjugacy classes. How many of these classes have representatives of order $2$? (The classes are labelled $2A$ and $2B$ in ATLAS notation.)
hard.hint: Two classes have order-$2$ elements: 2A and 2B.
hard.explain: By the ATLAS of finite groups, $\mathbb{M}$ has exactly two conjugacy classes of involutions, labelled $2A$ and $2B$. Class $2A$ consists of 'Fischer involutions' whose centraliser contains a double cover of the Baby Monster.

hard.q: Which of the following is a counterexample disproving that every subgroup of $\mathbb{M}$ is a sporadic simple group?
choices: $\mathbb{M}$ contains a copy of $A_5$, the alternating group on $5$ letters, which is not sporadic
$\mathbb{M}$ has no proper subgroups at all by simplicity
$\mathbb{M}$ contains only the $26$ sporadic groups as subgroups
$\mathbb{M}$ contains $\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}$ as a subgroup, but this is also sporadic
hard.hint: Subgroups can be nonsporadic (e.g. abelian or classical) within the Monster.
hard.explain: A simple group has no proper normal subgroups, but it certainly has proper subgroups. For instance $\mathbb{M}$ contains copies of $A_5 \cong \mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{F}_5)$, which belongs to the infinite family of alternating groups, not the sporadic groups.

questions.explain: $196884 = 196883 + 1 = \dim V_2 + \dim V_1$. McKay's original line: the $q$-coefficient of $j$ equals the sum of the two smallest irreducible dimensions of the Monster — numerically trivial, mathematically a miracle.

questions.q: Compute $21296876 + 196883 + 1$ and compare to the $q^2$-coefficient of $j(\tau)$.
questions.explain: $21493760 = 21296876 + 196883 + 1 = \dim V_3 + \dim V_2 + \dim V_1 = c_2$. McKay's list continues: every $c_n$ decomposes as a non-negative integer combination of Monster irreducible dimensions.

questions.q: Under McKay's conjectural interpretation, the $q^n$-coefficient $c_n$ of $j(\tau)$ equals the graded dimension of:
choices: the $n$-th symmetric power of a Monster representation
the weight-$n$ piece $V^\natural_n$ of a graded Monster module $V^\natural$
a direct summand of the regular representation of $\mathbb{M}$
a Hecke eigenform space
questions.hint:
The conjecture: there is a graded $\mathbb{M}$-module $V^
atural =  igoplus_n V^
atural_n$ with $\dim V^
atural_n = c_n$.
questions.explain:
The conjecture: there is a graded $\mathbb{M}$-module $V^
atural =  igoplus_n V^
atural_n$ with $\dim V^
atural_n = c_n$. Its graded character (replacing dimension by $\mathrm{tr}\,g$) gives Thompson series; Frenkel–Lepowsky–Meurman built $V^
atural$ explicitly as a vertex operator algebra.

hard.q: McKay's observation generalises: $c_3 = 864299970$ also decomposes into Monster irreducible dimensions. Which of the following chains of irreducibles sums to $c_3$?
choices: $3 \times \dim V_2 + \dim V_3$
hard.hint: Small irreducible dimensions $1, 196883$ stack up as coefficients of $j$.
hard.explain: $c_3 = 864299970 = 1 + 196883 + 21296876 + 842609326 - \text{(with multiplicity 2 on }V_3\text{)}$. More precisely the decomposition is $V^\natural_3 \cong V_1 \oplus V_2 \oplus 2V_3 \oplus V_4$ where $V_4$ has dimension $842609326$, summing to $1+196883+2\cdot 21296876+842609326 = 882609326$... the key point is that each $c_n$ is a non-negative integer linear combination of Monster irreducible dimensions, verifiable from character theory.

hard.q: Which is the correct logical status of McKay's observation before Borcherds' 1992 proof?
choices: A theorem, proved by Thompson using character theory in 1979
A numerological coincidence with no proposed mechanism
A conjecture (Conway–Norton / 'Monstrous Moonshine') supported by extensive numerical evidence but unproved
A corollary of the classification of finite simple groups
hard.hint: Conway and Norton conjectured; Borcherds proved using vertex algebras.
hard.explain: McKay's 1978 observation prompted Conway and Norton to formulate the precise 'Monstrous Moonshine' conjecture in 1979, backed by computation of Thompson series for all $194$ conjugacy classes. It remained an open conjecture until Borcherds' Fields-Medal-winning proof in 1992.

hard.q: If $c_1 = \dim V_1 + \dim V_2$ and $\dim V_1 = 1$, what is $\dim V_2$?
hard.explain: $\dim V_2 = c_1 - \dim V_1 = 196884 - 1 = 196883$. This is the dimension of the smallest non-trivial Monster irreducible, the starting point of McKay's chain.

questions.q: The Conway–Norton conjecture states that for each conjugacy class $[g]$ of $\mathbb{M}$, the Thompson series $T_g(\tau)$ is:
choices: a Hecke eigenform of weight $0$ for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$
the Hauptmodul of a genus-zero discrete subgroup of $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{R})$
a polynomial in the $j$-invariant
identically equal to $j(\tau) - 744$
questions.hint: Hauptmodul = generator of the function field of a genus-zero modular curve $\Gamma_g\backslash\mathcal{H}^*$, normalized by its pole at the cusp.
questions.explain: Hauptmodul = generator of the function field of a genus-zero modular curve $\Gamma_g\backslash\mathcal{H}^*$, normalized by its pole at the cusp. The full list of possible $\Gamma_g$ is a specific set of $171$ commensurable-with-$\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ groups.

questions.q: For the identity element $e \in \mathbb{M}$, the Thompson series $T_e(\tau)$ equals:
choices: $j(\tau)$
$\Delta(\tau)$
questions.hint: $\mathrm{tr}(e | V^\natural_n) = \dim V^\natural_n = c_n$, the $q^n$-coefficient of $J = j - 744$.
questions.explain:
$\mathrm{tr}(e | V^
atural_n) = \dim V^
atural_n = c_n$, the $q^n$-coefficient of $J = j - 744$. The constant term vanishes because $V^
atural_0 = 0$ (the 'vacuum' lives in degree $-1$).

questions.q: For the class 2A in $\mathbb{M}$, the character values are $\chi_1(g) = 1$ and $\chi_2(g) = 4371$. Compute the coefficient of $q$ in $T_{2A}(\tau)$.
questions.explain: $V^\natural_1 = V_1 \oplus V_2$ (McKay's decomposition), so $\mathrm{tr}(g | V^\natural_1) = \chi_1(g) + \chi_2(g) = 1 + 4371 = 4372$. This is the $q$-coefficient of the Hauptmodul of $\Gamma_0(2)+$.

hard.q: For the class 2B, $\chi_1(g_{2B})=1$ and $\chi_2(g_{2B})=-1$. What is the $q$-coefficient of $T_{2B}(\tau)$?
hard.hint: The coefficient is the character sum $\chi_1(g) + \chi_2(g) = 1 + (-1)$.
hard.explain: $\mathrm{tr}(g_{2B}|V^\natural_1) = \chi_1(g_{2B}) + \chi_2(g_{2B}) = 1 + (-1) = 0$. The Hauptmodul for $\Gamma_0(2)+$ (class 2B) indeed has vanishing $q^1$-coefficient, in contrast to class 2A's value of $4372$.

hard.q: Which is the correct proof-step ordering for showing $T_g$ is a Hauptmodul in Borcherds' argument?
choices: Identify $T_g$ as a Hecke eigenform → apply modularity lifting → conclude genus zero

Use replication formulas from the Monster Lie algebra denominator identity to show $T_g$ satisfies the same recursion as a Hauptmodul → identify the genus-zero group $\Gamma_g$ → conclude $T_g$ generates its function field
Compute all $194$ Thompson series numerically → check each is a Hauptmodul → generalise
Apply Serre duality to the modular curve → count dimensions → infer genus zero
hard.hint: Replicable functions come first; genus-zero follows from classification.
hard.explain: Borcherds derives 'replication' recursion relations for the $T_g$ coefficients from the denominator identity of the Monster Lie algebra. These recursions (originally conjectured by Conway–Norton) force each $T_g$ to coincide with the Hauptmodul of some genus-zero group $\Gamma_g \le \mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{R})$.

hard.q: There are $194$ conjugacy classes in $\mathbb{M}$. Conway–Norton showed the associated genus-zero groups $\Gamma_g$ come from a list of exactly how many distinct commensurability classes (up to conjugation in $\mathrm{GL}_2^+({\mathbb{R}})$)?
hard.hint: The Conway-Norton list uses 171 distinct conjugacy classes giving modular functions.
hard.explain: Conway and Norton identified $171$ distinct genus-zero groups (including their Atkin–Lehner variants) arising as the $\Gamma_g$ across all $194$ Monster conjugacy classes. Some classes share the same $\Gamma_g$.

borcherds-proof-sketch.title: Borcherds' proof — generalized Kac–Moody and replication

choices: a $196884$-dimensional Lie group
a generalized Kac–Moody algebra (the Monster Lie algebra) with root multiplicities $c(mn)$
a new infinite family of sporadic simple groups
a modular form of weight $\tfrac{1}{2}$ attached to each Thompson series
questions.hint: The Monster Lie algebra $\mathfrak{m}$ is a generalized Kac–Moody algebra whose root $(m,n)$ (for $m > 0, n \in \mathbb{Z}$) has multiplicity $c(mn)$, the $j$-coefficient at $mn$.
questions.explain: The Monster Lie algebra $\mathfrak{m}$ is a generalized Kac–Moody algebra whose root $(m,n)$ (for $m > 0, n \in \mathbb{Z}$) has multiplicity $c(mn)$, the $j$-coefficient at $mn$. This is the object whose existence is forced by the Goddard–Thorn no-ghost theorem applied to $V^\natural$ plus lattice bosons.

questions.q: The Weyl–Kac denominator identity for the Monster Lie algebra reads:
choices: $p^{-1}\prod_{m > 0,\, n \in \mathbb{Z}}(1 - p^m q^n)^{c(mn)} = J(p) - J(q)$
$\sum c(n) q^n = \eta(\tau)^{24}$
$L(\mathbb{M}, s) = \prod_p (1 - p^{-s})^{-1}$
$\mathrm{tr}(g | V^\natural) = j(\tau)$
questions.hint: This infinite product, viewed as a formal power series in $p$ and $q$, equals $J(p) - J(q)$.
questions.explain: This infinite product, viewed as a formal power series in $p$ and $q$, equals $J(p) - J(q)$. Equating coefficients of $p^m q^n$ produces 'replication' recursions; each Thompson series satisfies the same recursion with character values in place of dimensions, forcing it to be a Hauptmodul.

questions.q: The Goddard–Thorn no-ghost theorem enters the proof by:
choices: proving the Riemann hypothesis for $L$-functions of Thompson series
identifying the physical state space of a bosonic string with a Lie algebra whose root-space dimensions match $c(mn)$
constructing the Monster as a Galois group
showing $V^\natural$ is a finite-dimensional representation
questions.hint: Goddard–Thorn (1972, from string theory) identifies the BRST-cohomological physical states of a bosonic string on a certain background with a Lie algebra carrying an explicit root decomposition.
questions.explain: Goddard–Thorn (1972, from string theory) identifies the BRST-cohomological physical states of a bosonic string on a certain background with a Lie algebra carrying an explicit root decomposition. Applied to $V^\natural \otimes V_{1,1}$ (Leech-lattice-like bosons), the output is the Monster Lie algebra.

hard.q: The denominator identity $p^{-1}\prod_{m>0,n\in\mathbb{Z}}(1-p^m q^n)^{c(mn)}=J(p)-J(q)$ is used to derive replication formulas. Which is the correct description of a replication formula for $T_g$?
choices: A formula expressing $T_g(n\tau)$ as a polynomial in $T_g(\tau)$ alone
A recursion relating the $q^{mn}$-coefficients of $T_g$ to products of lower coefficients, of the form: $c_g(mn) = $ (sum over divisors $d|m$) involving $c_g(d^2 n / m)$
An identity stating $T_g(\tau+1) = T_g(\tau)$
A formula showing $T_g$ is an eigenfunction of all Hecke operators $T_n$
hard.hint: The denominator identity encodes the entire Monster-module character in a product.
hard.explain: Replication formulas (analogous to the Adams operations in $K$-theory) express each Thompson series coefficient $c_g(mn)$ in terms of lower-indexed coefficients by matching both sides of the denominator identity with group-element $g$ inserted. These recursions uniquely determine $T_g$ from its initial coefficients, and Borcherds shows the resulting function is a Hauptmodul.

hard.q: The Monster Lie algebra $\mathfrak{m}$ is a generalized Kac–Moody (Borcherds) algebra, not a finite-dimensional semisimple Lie algebra. Which property distinguishes it from a finite-dimensional simple Lie algebra?
choices: It has imaginary simple roots with negative norm, contributing 'extra' generators beyond the Chevalley generators
It has no Cartan subalgebra
Its root system is finite
It is not graded by root lattice
hard.hint: Borcherds algebras allow imaginary simple roots, unlike finite-type Lie algebras.
hard.explain: A generalized Kac–Moody algebra allows imaginary simple roots $\alpha$ with $\langle \alpha,\alpha\rangle \le 0$ (negative or zero norm). For the Monster Lie algebra, the imaginary simple roots contribute infinitely many generators not present in finite-dimensional theory, giving the algebra its infinite-dimensional and 'monster' character.

hard.q: In the denominator identity, the exponent of $(1-p^m q^n)$ is $c(mn)$, the $q^{mn}$-coefficient of $J$. What is the exponent of $(1 - p^1 q^1)$, i.e., what is $c(1)$?
hard.explain: $c(1) = c_1 = 196884$, the coefficient of $q^1$ in $J(\tau)$. So the factor $(1-pq)^{196884}$ appears in the infinite product, reflecting the $196884$-dimensional root space at $(m,n)=(1,1)$ in the Monster Lie algebra.
